label: Rambling advice, sharpened by Franklin
author: franklin
originalText: I think it is usually a pretty good idea to try to save at least a little bit of money when you can.

iterations.inputText: I think it is usually a pretty good idea to try to save at least a little bit of money when you can.
iterations.outputText: Saving some money is generally a good idea.

critique.comment: This opening is flat and lacks the aphoristic snap of Franklin's prose.
Too modern and conversational. Franklin is direct and prescriptive, not suggestive.

nextInstructions: Adopt an aphoristic or proverbial structure.
Use balanced clauses or parallel phrasing (e.g., 'A penny saved is a penny earned').
Employ simple, concrete nouns and strong, active verbs.
State the advice as a timeless truth, not a personal opinion or suggestion.
Aim for a sentence length between 12 and 20 words.
Eliminate modern conversational softeners like 'generally' or 'a good idea'.

iterations.inputText: Saving some money is generally a good idea.
iterations.outputText: A prudent man saves his coin; a fool squanders his fortune.

critique.comment: This introduces a new idea (losing a 'fortune') not present in the original, slightly reducing fidelity.

nextInstructions: Maintain the aphoristic, balanced structure.
Use simple, concrete nouns like 'coin' or 'penny'.
Employ parallel constructions (e.g., 'A prudent man...; a fool...').
Focus on the positive action of saving, not just the negative of squandering.
Ensure the meaning stays close to the original idea of saving 'a little bit'.
Keep sentences short and declarative.

iterations.inputText: A prudent man saves his coin; a fool squanders his fortune.
iterations.outputText: A wise man saves his penny. A fool spends his pound. Save a little now. You will have much later.

critique.span: A wise man saves his penny. A fool spends his pound.
critique.comment: This is two separate sentences. Franklin would use parallelism and connect them with a semicolon or conjunction.

critique.span: Save a little now. You will have much later.
critique.comment: Too choppy and simplistic. The previous draft's single, balanced sentence was more effective and aphoristic.
This is an anachronism. The previous draft's 'coin' and 'fortune' were more timeless and fitting.

nextInstructions: Combine short, choppy sentences into a single, balanced aphorism.
Use parallelism to structure your maxims (e.g., 'A prudent man saves X; a fool squanders Y').
Connect contrasting ideas with a semicolon or conjunction, not a full stop.
Choose timeless, concrete nouns like 'coin' or 'fortune' over specific, anachronistic currency like 'penny' or 'pound'.
Strive for a single, memorable sentence that encapsulates the entire idea.
Avoid breaking a single thought into multiple, simplistic sentences.

iterations.inputText: A wise man saves his penny. A fool spends his pound. Save a little now. You will have much later.
iterations.outputText: A prudent man hoards his coin; a spendthrift dissipates his fortune, for a little saved today becomes much hereafter.

critique.comment: Slightly too ornate. 'Fortune' feels grander than the typical Franklin scale of pennies and pounds.
A bit abstract. Franklin often prefers more concrete outcomes or simpler phrasing like 'grows great'.

nextInstructions: Use balanced, parallel clauses (e.g., 'A penny saved is a penny earned').
Employ simple, concrete nouns (e.g., 'penny', 'shilling', 'purse').
Construct short, memorable maxims or aphorisms.
Maintain a practical, advisory tone.
Connect actions directly to their future consequences.
Avoid overly ornate or abstract language; favor plain speech.
Aim for an average sentence length of around 14-20 words.

iterations.inputText: A prudent man hoards his coin; a spendthrift dissipates his fortune, for a little saved today becomes much hereafter.
iterations.outputText: A wise man keeps his shillings; a fool spends his pounds. For a farthing put aside now is a pound gained later.

critique.span: A wise man keeps his shillings; a fool spends his pounds.
critique.comment: Excellent parallel structure and aphoristic quality. Captures the voice perfectly.

critique.span: a farthing put aside now is a pound gained later
critique.comment: Strong, concrete imagery and a clear, practical maxim. Well done.

nextInstructions: Maintain the use of parallel structures and balanced maxims.
Continue using concrete, everyday nouns (shillings, pounds, farthings).
Keep sentences concise and declarative.
Ensure the core message remains practical and offers clear advice.
The current voice is nearly perfect. Focus on consistency in future rewrites.
